risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: The study explicitly describes a computer-generated randomization method (urn randomization), which indicates a truly random allocation sequence, even though it was stratified/balanced on baseline factors.

Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: This describes the randomization/sequence generation method, not how allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants. The report does not explicitly mention a concealment mechanism such as central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes. Therefore, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All participants received twice weekly group sessions (eight total) that were manualized and delivered by instructors experienced in MT ... or certified in FFS respectively" and "Each participant received a meditation practice CD" / "Each participant received a practice CD of cessation techniques." Comment: The interventions were clearly different behavioral programs delivered by different therapists, so participants and personnel delivering treatment would necessarily know group assignment; no blinding of participants/personnel is described. The main outcomes included self-reported smoking and abstinence-related behavior, which are plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation and treatment expectations in a behavioral trial. The paper also notes, "As subject expectancy of receiving treatment can have non-specific effects on smoking..." supporting that outcomes could be affected by lack of blinding.

Quote: "Self-reported smoking was assessed at in-person weekly visits by a research assistant who was not involved in treatment delivery via the Timeline Follow Back method (TLFB)... Self-reported abstinence was assessed using TLFB and verified by an exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) measurement of ≤ 10 parts per million..." Comment: Outcome assessment was done by both patient self-report (the patient is the assessor for smoking and abstinence by TLFB) and a research assistant collecting those reports; the paper does not state that participants or the research assistant were blinded to allocation, and in this behavioral trial the intervention groups (mindfulness training vs Freedom From Smoking) would likely be known. The primary outcomes included subjective self-reported measures (one-week point prevalence abstinence by self-report and average number of cigarettes smoked/day). Although CO verification is objective, the study reports a mix of objective and subjective outcomes, and the unblinded/self-reported subjective outcomes are the most vulnerable. By the worst-outcome rule, this makes detection bias High risk.

Quote: "Of the 103 eligible individuals, 88 were randomized (see CONSORT diagram, Fig. 1)." "Longitudinal data were analyzed using intent-to-treat mixed effect regression models on the full sample of randomized subjects (minus one individual who was incarcerated after treatment and whose data were not allowed to be analyzed per Veteran’s Administration regulations)... Incomplete data were handled using casewise deletion, using all available data for parameter estimates." "Sixteen (eight in each group) did not complete baseline paperwork and were not exposed to treatment..." "The six, 12, and 17-week follow-up completion rates were 27 (82% of treatment-exposed individuals) and 33 (87%), 29 (88%) and 32 (84%), and 29 (88%) and 33 (87%) for MT and FFS respectively." Comment: The 16 participants who never started treatment (8/arm) are post-randomization losses, but they were balanced across groups. Among treatment-exposed participants, follow-up at the primary 17-week endpoint was 29/33 (88%) in MT and 33/38 (87%) in FFS, indicating low and well-balanced missing outcome data. The analysis also used ITT mixed-effects models on the randomized sample (except 1 incarcerated participant excluded for regulatory reasons), so there is positive evidence that incomplete outcome data were unlikely to bias the main outcome comparison.
